questions.question: Your team is standing up Real-Time Intelligence in Fabric and needs a single item that persists incoming telemetry long-term and can hold several KQL databases that share the same compute and storage allocation. Which item provides this?
questions.explanation: An Eventhouse is the Real-Time Intelligence storage item; it groups one or more KQL databases that share compute and storage. A KQL Queryset only stores and runs queries against an existing database, so it persists nothing itself. An Eventstream moves and shapes data but is not a durable analytical store, and a Lakehouse uses Delta/Parquet rather than the Kusto engine tuned for time-series and log data.

questions.question: Events arrive from an Azure Event Hub and must be dropped into a KQL database. You want to keep only records where the status field is not null before they land, and you must do this on a drag-and-drop canvas rather than writing Spark or pipeline code. Which item fits?

options.C: Dataflow Gen2 with a custom function
options.D: Data pipeline with a Copy activity and a filter expression

questions.explanation: Eventstream connects to streaming sources such as Azure Event Hubs and offers no-code transformations (including Filter) before routing to an Eventhouse destination. A Copy activity in a pipeline can move data but its mapping does not provide a streaming row-level filter on the canvas, and it is batch-oriented. Dataflow Gen2 and Spark notebooks are not no-code streaming tools; the notebook in particular is code-first.

questions.question: In KQL you must keep only the rows whose Severity equals "Critical" and discard the rest. Which operator does this?
questions.explanation: where evaluates a predicate per row and keeps the matching rows. extend adds a computed column without removing rows, project chooses which columns to output (not which rows), and distinct removes duplicate rows rather than applying a predicate.

questions.question: An Eventstream must deliver processed events to two places at once: a Lakehouse for batch ML and an Eventhouse for KQL dashboards, all from one source. Which TWO statements about Eventstream destinations are correct? Select all that apply.

options.A: A single Eventstream can attach multiple destinations that receive data simultaneously without interfering with each other
options.B: Adding a second destination forces the first destination to be removed
options.C: A Lakehouse destination stores events as CSV files only
options.D: The Eventhouse destination offers a direct-ingestion mode and an event-processing-before-ingestion mode
options.E: Each destination requires its own separate Eventstream item

questions.explanation: An Eventstream supports fan-out: a single Eventstream can attach multiple destinations that receive data simultaneously without interfering with each other, so the claim that each destination requires its own separate Eventstream is false, as is the claim that adding a second destination removes the first. The Eventhouse destination genuinely exposes two ingestion modes, direct ingestion and event processing before ingestion. The Lakehouse destination writes Delta Lake (Parquet), not CSV files only.

questions.question: A dashboard repeatedly runs the same heavy aggregation over a fast-growing table. You want the engine to keep the aggregated result current as new rows arrive, so the dashboard reads a small precomputed set. Which KQL object should you create?

options.B: A stored function returning the aggregate
options.C: An update policy targeting a second table

questions.explanation: A materialized view incrementally maintains an aggregation so repeated reads hit precomputed results. A stored function re-computes the aggregate at every query, giving no maintained result. An update policy transforms rows at ingestion into a target table but does not itself maintain a rolling aggregate, and row-level security governs access, not performance.

questions.question: Raw records arrive in a KQL table with one packed string column. You want each new row reshaped into typed columns automatically as it is ingested, written into a curated table, with no scheduled job. Which mechanism is designed for this?
questions.explanation: An update policy runs a transformation query whenever data lands in the source table and writes the result to a target table, so reshaping happens at ingestion with no scheduler. A materialized view aggregates existing data rather than reshaping each incoming row into a curated table; continuous export moves data out of the database; the caching policy only governs which data sits in hot cache.

questions.question: Operations staff want to be notified and have a remediation pipeline started automatically whenever a monitored metric breaches a threshold in streaming data. Which Fabric item is purpose-built to watch the data and act?
questions.explanation: Activator monitors streams or KQL data for conditions and takes actions such as sending alerts or starting a Fabric pipeline or Power Automate flow. A Real-Time Dashboard only visualizes and requires a human to notice; a KQL Queryset stores queries; an Eventhouse stores data but performs no condition-based actions.

questions.question: Which TWO statements correctly describe the relationship between the retention policy and the caching policy in a KQL database? Select all that apply.

options.A: The caching period must be less than or equal to the retention period
options.B: Hot-cached data sits on local SSD for faster queries while colder data stays in standard storage but is still queryable
options.C: Reducing the caching period removes data permanently from the database
options.D: The retention policy (SoftDeletePeriod) determines how long data stays queryable before automatic removal
options.E: The caching policy determines how long data remains queryable

questions.explanation: The caching policy decides what is held on fast local SSD as hot for quicker queries while colder data stays in standard storage but remains fully queryable. The retention policy (SoftDeletePeriod) controls how long data stays queryable before it is automatically removed, so it governs queryable lifespan and eventual deletion. The statement that reducing the caching period removes data permanently is false, because shrinking the cache only moves data to standard storage and never deletes it; queryability is governed by retention, not by the caching policy; and although the caching period being less than or equal to the retention period is a real configuration constraint, it describes a limit rather than what each policy fundamentally does.

questions.question: A logistics firm streams truck GPS telemetry into an Eventstream. The same events must be retained raw in a Lakehouse for ML training and also land in an Eventhouse for live KQL dashboards. They want the fewest moving parts. What should they do?

options.A: Build two Eventstreams, each reading the source independently
options.B: Land events in the Lakehouse, then run a notebook every few minutes to push them to the Eventhouse
options.C: Attach both a Lakehouse destination and an Eventhouse destination to the one Eventstream
options.D: Land events in the Eventhouse, then schedule continuous export to copy them into the Lakehouse

questions.explanation: One Eventstream can fan out to multiple destinations, so attaching both targets delivers the same events to each with no extra copy job. Continuous export and a polling notebook add latency and a second moving part, and two Eventstreams read the source twice and duplicate ingestion cost.

questions.question: A retailer must alert the operations team and kick off a remediation pipeline whenever any store's error rate stays above 5% across a rolling window. Events already flow through an Eventstream into an Eventhouse. They want minimal custom code. Which approach is best?

options.A: Pin the metric to a Real-Time Dashboard tile and have staff watch for breaches
options.B: Configure a Dataflow Gen2 that refreshes conditionally and emails on breach
options.C: Create an Activator that triggers on the condition and runs both the alert and the pipeline
options.D: Add a continuous export that writes breach rows to a folder a logic app polls
options.E: Run a KQL query from a scheduled notebook and send mail through SMTP code

questions.explanation: Activator is built for condition detection on live data and can both alert and start a pipeline with no custom code. A dashboard needs a human watching; a scheduled notebook adds polling latency and bespoke mail code; Dataflow Gen2 is batch and not event-driven; a continuous-export-plus-poller chain adds latency and external glue. Only Activator meets the low-code, low-latency requirement.

questions.question: Analysts query average temperature per device per hour over the last day, repeatedly, against a raw KQL table, and dashboard latency is unacceptable. Which TWO changes would reduce that latency? Select all that apply.

options.A: Extend the retention period on the raw table
options.B: Lower the caching period below the queried range so less data is held hot
options.C: Ensure the queried time range stays within the table's hot caching period
options.D: Create a materialized view aggregating average temperature by device and hour
options.E: Add an update policy that copies every raw row into an identical second table

questions.explanation: Creating a materialized view that aggregates average temperature by device and hour precomputes the result so dashboards read a small maintained set instead of scanning raw rows, and ensuring the queried time range stays within the table's hot caching period serves those reads from fast SSD. Extending the retention period only keeps more data without speeding aggregation; adding an update policy that copies every raw row into an identical second table still requires the same aggregation work at query time; and lowering the caching period below the queried range pushes data to slower cold storage and worsens latency.

questions.question: About 2% of incoming events fail schema validation. Valid events should land in the Eventhouse and the malformed ones must be captured separately for later inspection rather than discarded. What is the best Eventstream design?

options.A: Add a Filter that produces a derived stream of valid events to the Eventhouse and route the non-matching events to a Lakehouse destination
options.B: Turn off schema enforcement so all events land together in the Eventhouse
options.C: Send everything to one Eventhouse destination and let bad rows be rejected
options.D: Add an update policy on the Eventhouse table that deletes invalid rows on ingestion

questions.explanation: A Filter operation in the Eventstream branches the data; matching events become a derived stream to the Eventhouse and the remainder can be routed to a Lakehouse, preserving both sets. Letting rows be rejected or deleting them via an update policy loses the malformed data needed for inspection, and disabling enforcement mixes good and bad data with no separation.

questions.question: A security team must keep 6 months of logs all queryable, but only the most recent 7 days need sub-second interactive response. They want to minimize cost. How should the KQL database policies be set?
questions.explanation: Retention must be 6 months so all data stays queryable, and caching set to 7 days keeps only recent data hot for sub-second response while older data remains queryable in cheaper standard storage. Caching for the full 6 months wastes SSD cost; disabling caching slows even recent queries; setting retention to 7 days would delete the older data the team must keep.

questions.question: Forensic analysts occasionally need fast queries against a single past day that sits well outside the hot caching window, but the team does not want to widen the cache for all old data or export it. Which Eventhouse capability addresses this?

options.A: Increase the retention period so the day becomes hot again
options.B: Create a continuous export of the cold data to a Lakehouse and query there
options.C: Define a hot window in the caching policy for the targeted past period
options.D: Switch the table's caching policy to Unlimited

questions.explanation: Hot windows let you mark specific past time ranges of cold data to be cached and queried quickly without widening the cache for everything. Raising retention does not change what is cached; continuous export adds a separate copy and pipeline; setting caching to Unlimited would cache all data and defeats the cost goal.

questions.question: Incoming events from an Event Hub must be enriched in-flight with attributes from a small, slowly-changing device reference set before they reach the Eventhouse. Which TWO are valid ways to achieve enrichment in Real-Time Intelligence? Select all that apply.

options.A: Drop the reference set and hardcode the attribute values into each dashboard tile
options.B: Store the reference data inside the Eventstream as a static source and never update it
options.C: Land raw events and resolve the attributes at query time with a KQL join against the reference table
options.D: Use a Dataflow Gen2 to join the streaming events with the reference data continuously
options.E: Use the Eventstream Join transformation to combine the live stream with the reference data

questions.explanation: Using the Eventstream Join transformation combines the live stream with the reference data in-flight, and landing raw events then resolving the attributes at query time with a KQL join against the reference table is a legitimate alternative when pre-joining is not required. Using a Dataflow Gen2 to join continuously is wrong because Dataflow Gen2 is batch and not a streaming enricher; storing the reference data inside the Eventstream as a static source that never updates ignores the slowly-changing requirement; and hardcoding the attribute values into each dashboard tile is unmaintainable.

questions.question: Multiple dashboards and querysets need the same parameterized logic, for example top-N error codes for a supplied time range, without each copying the query text. What should be created in the KQL database?

options.A: A separate KQL database per dashboard

questions.explanation: A stored (user-defined) function takes parameters and can be invoked from many queries, dashboards, and querysets, centralizing the logic. A materialized view maintains a fixed aggregate and is not parameterized; an update policy runs at ingestion, not on demand with arguments; one database per dashboard fragments the data and duplicates effort.

questions.question: Stakeholders want a Real-Time Dashboard where selecting a region updates every tile at once, without editing individual queries each time. What is the maintainable way to build this?

options.A: Define a dashboard parameter and reference it in each tile's query
options.B: Embed the region as a literal in every tile's query
options.C: Use an Activator rule to swap the region filter
options.D: Create one dashboard per region and switch between them

questions.explanation: Real-Time Dashboards support parameters; referencing a single parameter across tiles lets one selection filter them all. Per-region dashboards and hardcoded literals do not respond to a single selection and multiply maintenance, and Activator triggers actions on data rather than filtering dashboard tiles.

questions.question:
What does this KQL query return?

Telemetry
| where Timestamp > ago(1h)
| summarize AvgTemp = avg(Temperature) by DeviceId, bin(Timestamp, 5m)
| where AvgTemp > 80
| order by Timestamp desc

options.A: The hottest single reading per device in the last hour
options.B: Per-device 5-minute average temperatures over the last hour, keeping only bins above 80, newest first
options.C: A count of devices that reported in the last hour
options.D: Every raw row from the last hour where Temperature exceeds 80

questions.explanation: The query filters to the last hour, groups by DeviceId and 5-minute bins computing avg(Temperature), keeps bins whose average exceeds 80, and sorts newest first, producing per-device 5-minute average temperatures over the last hour, keeping only bins above 80, newest first. It returns averaged bins, not every raw row above 80, not the hottest single reading per device, and not a count of devices.

questions.question: You need per StatusCode the request count for the last 24 hours, showing only codes with more than 1000 requests, sorted most frequent first. Which query is correct?

options.A: Requests | where Timestamp > ago(24h) | summarize Count = count() by StatusCode | top 1000 by Count desc
options.B: Requests | where Count > 1000 | summarize count() by StatusCode | order by Timestamp desc
options.C: Requests | where Timestamp > ago(24h) | project StatusCode | summarize Count = count() | where Count > 1000
options.D: Requests | summarize Count = count() by StatusCode | where Timestamp > ago(24h) | order by Count desc
options.E: Requests | where Timestamp > ago(24h) | summarize Count = count() by StatusCode | where Count > 1000 | order by Count desc

questions.explanation: The correct query filters to the last 24 hours, aggregates count() per StatusCode, keeps only groups whose Count exceeds 1000, then orders by Count descending. The query using top 1000 by Count returns the 1000 highest-count codes rather than codes whose count exceeds 1000. The query that references Timestamp after summarize fails because that column no longer exists once the data is aggregated. The query that filters on Count before it is computed and orders by the dropped Timestamp column is invalid. The query that projects StatusCode then summarizes count() without a by clause collapses to a single total and loses the per-StatusCode grouping.
